conventional_view: Most California public-agency lawyers would conclude that a serial (hub-and-spoke) meeting under Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(1) occurred or, at minimum, created substantial exposure requiring remediation — because the statute's "series of communications" and "intermediary" language does not require that members intend to communicate with each other or know the mechanism is relaying information; it turns on whether a majority's substantive views/positions were actually exchanged en route to a collective concurrence, and here CivicAssist functioned as the hub, actually transmitting one member's stated concerns and positions to others. The recommended course would be to treat this as a violation (or unacceptable risk of one): respond substantively to the § 54960.1 cure-and-correct demand, restart the Harbor Yards hearing with a clean, disclosed process, avoid framing this as individual misconduct absent intent, and immediately adopt/enforce a policy disabling cross-member shared AI memory going forward.
disfavored_position: No serial meeting occurred: because no councilmember directed, knew of, or intended any communication of views to another member, and no member received information understanding it came from a colleague, there was no "communication" by or attributable to any member within the meaning of § 54952.2(b)(1); the AI's autonomous aggregation of prior inputs is not an "intermediary" carrying a member's message but an internal data-processing artifact more akin to (or within) the staff-briefing safe harbor of § 54952.2(b)(2), such that no cure-and-correct obligation arises, the hearing may proceed as scheduled, and no policy violation occurred requiring anything beyond prospective technical/configuration changes.
